title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: Six paragraphs have been removed from the text. Choose the correct paragraph (A-G) to fill each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.title: The Art of the Invisible: The Ethics of Restoration
content.text: Every time a masterpiece like the Sistine Chapel or a Rembrandt portrait is 'restored,' a fierce debate erupts in the art world. For some, restoration is a vital act of rescue, removing the grime of centuries to reveal the artist's original vision. For others, it is an act of vandalism that strips away the history and soul of a work. || (41) || [41] This central conflict revolves around the concept of 'the artist's intent' [/41]. But how can we know what an artist intended five hundred years ago? Was a painting meant to be bright and vibrant, or did the artist use subtle glazes that were always intended to look dark and atmospheric? || (42) || [42] One of the most controversial examples of this occurred during the cleaning of Michelangelo’s frescoes [/42]. When the layers of soot and old wax were removed, critics were shocked by the 'candy-colored' palette that emerged. Many argued that the restorers had removed Michelangelo’s own final touches of shadow. || (43) || [43] To avoid such disasters, modern restorers have adopted a policy of 'minimal intervention' [/43]. The goal today is not to make a painting look 'new,' but to stabilize it and prevent further decay. Any additions made by the restorer must be 'reversible,' meaning they can be removed in the future without damaging the original paint. || (44) || [44] This approach is supported by a suite of high-tech tools [/44]. Using X-rays, infrared reflectography, and chemical analysis, restorers can see beneath the surface of a painting, identifying previous repairs and understanding the exact composition of the original pigments. || (45) || [45] However, technology cannot solve the more philosophical questions of restoration [/45]. For instance, if a 17th-century painting was altered in the 19th century by a famous owner, should that alteration be kept as part of the work's history, or removed to return it to its 'pure' state? || (46) || [46] There is no simple answer, and every case is unique [/46]. Ultimately, restoration is a balance between science and art, a delicate dance between the past and the present. The restorer must be part detective, part scientist, and part artist, all while remaining as invisible as possible.

paragraphs.A: [42] These technical breakthroughs have removed much of the guesswork from the process [/42]. We can now determine if a specific brushstroke was made by the master himself or by a later, less-skilled apprentice.
paragraphs.B: [41] This question is further complicated by the natural aging of materials [/41]. Varnish yellows over time, and certain pigments fade or change color when exposed to light, meaning the painting we see today is never truly the one the artist finished.
paragraphs.C: [44] It is a philosophy that prioritizes the integrity of the object over its aesthetic appeal [/44]. It acknowledges that every time we touch a work of art, we risk losing something irreplaceable.
paragraphs.D: [43] The backlash from this project changed the field forever [/43]. It forced restorers to be more transparent about their methods and more cautious about the use of powerful chemical solvents.
paragraphs.E: [45] These decisions are often driven by fashion and the values of the time [/45]. What one generation considers a 'correction' of a previous mistake, the next generation might see as a historical tragedy.
paragraphs.F: [46] This is why the best restoration is often said to be the one you can't see [/46]. If a viewer's first reaction is 'Look how well this has been restored,' then the restorer has, in a sense, failed to respect the work.
paragraphs.G: Artificial intelligence is now being used to predict how a painting might have looked centuries ago. By analyzing the artist's style and the degradation patterns, AI can create a digital 'reconstruction' for historians to study.

questions.explanation: B profundiza en la dificultad de conocer la intención del artista debido al envejecimiento natural de los materiales (barniz, pigmentos).
A se refiere a los 'technical breakthroughs' que eliminan las conjeturas, permitiendo distinguir entre el maestro y el aprendiz.
D se refiere al 'backlash' (reacción negativa) del proyecto de la Capilla Sixtina, que cambió las políticas del campo de la restauración.
C define la filosofía de 'mínima intervención' como una prioridad por la integridad del objeto sobre su estética.
E discute cómo las decisiones de restauración están influenciadas por las 'modas' y los valores de cada época.
F concluye con la idea de que la mejor restauración es la invisible, conectando con el cierre del texto sobre el papel del restaurador.
